items.title: U.S. and Israel strikes on Iran trigger the first market shock
items.description: Reuters reported that the strikes on Iran put Gulf oil producers on edge and triggered fears around shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
items.impact: This is the starting point of the crisis chain. It is the first event that turned a geopolitical clash into a global oil-market problem.

items.title: Investors and crude producers rush to lock in the oil spike
items.description: Reuters said the first trading day after the strikes brought a record wave of futures and options activity as producers tried to hedge the sudden price jump.
items.impact: This shows how a military strike becomes a pricing event. Futures and hedging react before retail fuel settles into a new band.

items.title: Oil resumes its climb as Iran supply fears refuse to fade
items.description: Reuters reported that the brief market respite ended and Brent stayed above the key $100 level as the Strait of Hormuz remained effectively shut.
items.impact: This matters because it shows the crisis did not spike once and vanish. The market kept pricing supply risk across weeks, not days.

items.title: Civil servants living over 8km from office can WFH
items.description: The Star reported that civil servants who live more than 8km from their office can work from home under the new flexibility rule.
items.impact: This is a meaningful Malaysia-side response because it shows the crisis period affecting daily operations beyond fuel prices alone.

items.title: Trump’s ceasefire deadline turns the Strait of Hormuz into a market countdown
items.description: Reuters coverage on the eve of the deadline described Iran as defiant while Trump warned that the U.S. would escalate if the Strait of Hormuz was not reopened safely.
items.impact: This is the point where the market started pricing the countdown itself, not just the earlier conflict.

items.title: EIA says Brent averaged about $103 in March and briefly neared $128 on April 2
items.description: The EIA’s first-quarter price review said the Middle East conflict pushed Brent sharply higher, with the quarter ending at $118 and the April 2 peak near $128.
items.impact: This gives the timeline a concrete market marker. It shows the crisis was not just a headline shock; it rewired oil pricing across the entire quarter.

items.title: A two-week ceasefire pushes oil back below $100
items.description: Reuters reported that oil futures fell below $100 after the U.S. and Iran agreed to suspend strikes and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
items.impact: This is the clearest relief rally in the sequence. It shows how fast prices can move when the market believes the shipping threat has eased.

items.title: MOF says pump prices still reflect the previous week's average cost
items.description: The ministry explained that Malaysia's pump prices lag the headline oil market because the weekly average cost is what enters the pricing formula.
items.impact: This is the local policy bridge between the global oil shock and the actual price drivers see in Malaysia.

items.title: Talks collapse, oil bounces back above $100
items.description: Reuters reported that oil prices bounced back above $100 after U.S.-Iran talks ended in stalemate, showing how quickly the relief rally could unwind.
items.impact: This is the moment the market proved the ceasefire was fragile. The risk premium came back before the blockade itself took effect.

items.title: Oil pushes back above $100 as the blockade starts
items.description: Reuters reported that oil jumped above $100 when the U.S. military said it would block ships leaving Iran’s ports, with immediate-delivery physical barrels in Europe trading much higher.
items.impact: This is the latest major escalation in the chain. It marks the point where the crisis moves from fear to direct supply pricing.

items.title: U.S. Navy begins active tanker interdictions forcing price volatility
items.description: Reports confirmed that U.S. naval forces actively interdicted tankers leaving Iranian ports under the President's orders. Successive come-and-back threats between parties pushed supply risk drastically higher.
items.impact: This marks actual military enforcement of the blockade, transforming theoretical political tension into concrete real-world supply disruption.
